item.title: Man sentenced to 10yrs for sexual assault and rape of a 16-year-old girl
item.description: A 49-year-old man has been sentenced to 10 years imprisonment for one count of sexual assault and one count of rape of a family member. The incident took place on the 1st of October 2025 to the 31st of October, 2025.
item.content:encoded:
A 49-year-old man has been sentenced to 10 years imprisonment for one count of sexual assault and one count of rape of a family member.

The incident took place on the 1st of October 2025 to the 31st of October, 2025.

The man had pleaded guilty on his own free will.

Labasa Magistrate Lee James Burnley says the 16-year-old girl had informed her grandmother, who reported the matter to the police.

During caution interview, the man admitted that he sexually assaulted and raped the complainant on a number of occasions.

He also told the police that he did that because he had admired the complainant for a long time.

The prosecution submits that his offending is made more serious because the complainant regarded him as her grandfather.

The complainant was diagnosed intellectual impairment and in her victim impact statement, she speaks of her shame and embarrassment.

Justice Burnley says what makes the offending more serious, including the complainant’s extreme vulnerability and breach of trust.

Justice Burnley encourages him to reflect at length on the inevitable harm caused to the complainant , and to engage with any intervention programmes that may be available during his period of incarceration.

A non-parole period of 6 years and 5 months has been set.

item.title: Journey home leads Brisbane family to discover their ancestry at the National Archives of Fiji
item.description: A family holiday to Fiji became a journey of discovery for the Khan family from Brisbane, who visited the National Archives of Fiji to trace their ancestry and learn more about their family's history. Safeen Khan, who was born and raised in Labasa before moving to Australia, visited the Archives with his wife, Nazia Ali, and their two children as part of a trip back home.
item.content:encoded:
A family holiday to Fiji became a journey of discovery for the Khan family from Brisbane, who visited the National Archives of Fiji to trace their ancestry and learn more about their family's history.

Safeen Khan, who was born and raised in Labasa before moving to Australia, visited the Archives with his wife, Nazia Ali, and their two children as part of a trip back home.

Speaking to fijivillage News, Khan says one of the main reasons for visiting the National Archives was to learn more about where their ancestors came from and to help their children better understand their roots.

Insert: Khan family - We enjoyed ourselves June 10

He says the family was delighted to discover information about their lineage, including details about their great-great-grandfathers.

Khan says the visit coincided with the National Archives' Open Day, giving the family a unique opportunity to see first-hand how historical documents are preserved and protected for future generations.

He describes the experience as both informative and enjoyable, particularly for the children, who were fascinated by the conservation process and the care taken to preserve records that are more than a century old.

Khan says it was special to return home and see that these important records still exist, allowing families like his to connect with their history and pass that knowledge on to future generations.

His wife, Nazia Ali, echoed those sentiments, saying it was exciting to learn more about the history contained within the Archives and the work that goes into preserving it.

item.title: 24-Hour Safe Shelter opens in Western Division for survivors of gender-based violence
item.description: Women and girls affected by gender-based violence in the Western Division will now have access to a 24-hour safe shelter following a partnership between the Ministry of Women and Empower Pacific. The shelter will provide round-the-clock accommodation, crisis intervention, psychosocial support, counselling, referrals and other essential services for women, girls and individuals seeking protection a
item.content:encoded:
Women and girls affected by gender-based violence in the Western Division will now have access to a 24-hour safe shelter following a partnership between the Ministry of Women and Empower Pacific.

The shelter will provide round-the-clock accommodation, crisis intervention, psychosocial support, counselling, referrals and other essential services for women, girls and individuals seeking protection and assistance from violence.

Permanent Secretary for Women, Selina Kuruleca says the partnership reflects the Ministry's commitment to strengthening protection services for women and girls and improving access to quality support for survivors of violence.

Minister Sashi Kiran says addressing gender-based violence requires a collective effort involving government agencies, civil society organisations, faith-based groups, development partners, communities and other stakeholders.

Kiran says partnerships such as this are critical in strengthening efforts to prevent violence and ensuring that women and girls can live free from abuse and harm.

She also acknowledged Empower Pacific for its continued commitment to providing professional and survivor-centred services to people affected by violence.

Kiran says Empower Pacific has been an important partner in supporting survivors and promoting the wellbeing of women and families.

The Ministry has also expressed gratitude to Soroptimist International for handing over the building that now serves as the safe shelter and acknowledged the support of LDS Charities towards the initiative.

The Ministry says the shelter is part of ongoing efforts under the Fiji National Action Plan to Prevent Violence Against All Women and Girls 2023–2028 and aims to strengthen services that protect the rights, safety and wellbeing of women, children, older persons, people living with disabilities and families throughout Fiji.

item.title: Blood drive underway at Nasinu Town Council today
item.description: The Nasinu Town Council is encouraging members of the public to help save lives by participating in a blood drive being held today at the Council's car park. The initiative comes as the Ministry of Health Blood Bank urgently requires blood donations of all types to help meet the growing demand for blood supplies across the country.
item.content:encoded:
The Nasinu Town Council is encouraging members of the public to help save lives by participating in a blood drive being held today at the Council's car park.

The initiative comes as the Ministry of Health Blood Bank urgently requires blood donations of all types to help meet the growing demand for blood supplies across the country.

The blood drive will run until 2pm, and people are being urged to bring along their family members and friends to support the life-saving cause.

For those who do not know their blood type, this is a great opportunity to find out, as you will receive information on your blood type and test results.

item.title: Nakasi alleged murder accused Laqeni recovering from surgery, absence excused
item.description: Epeli Laqeni, who is accused of allegedly killing his de facto partner in Nakasi last September, did not appear in court yesterday

item.title: Navtarang/Sargam show returns to Fiji Showcase with bigger entertainment and more prizes
item.description: Great news for entertainment lovers, as one of the biggest attractions at Fiji Showcase, the Navtarang/Sargam Show, is set to entertain crowds next Sunday. Senior Content Director of Communications Fiji Limited, Satya Nand says the show will run from 6pm to 8.30pm and will feature exciting entertainment, comedy, audience interaction and plenty of prizes.
item.content:encoded:
Great news for entertainment lovers, as one of the biggest attractions at Fiji Showcase, the Navtarang/Sargam Show, is set to entertain crowds next Sunday.

Senior Content Director of Communications Fiji Limited, Satya Nand says the show will run from 6pm to 8.30pm and will feature exciting entertainment, comedy, audience interaction and plenty of prizes.

Nand says the programme will include performances by the Shobna Chanel Dance Group, Vou Dance Fiji, the Navtarang team and the popular 5 to 6 Masala Mix team.

Insert: Nand on preparations 10th June HINDI

Fiji Showcase tickets can be purchased at the gate and are priced at $4 for adults and $3 for children.

He is encouraging everyone to come along, saying it is a show people cannot afford to miss.

item.title: Urgent action needed as we have largest cabinet in Fiji's history and more than $11B government debt - Dialogue Fiji Executive Director
item.description: We have the largest cabinet in Fiji's history, and more than $11 billion government debt, all of which is happening when the taxpa

item.title: Water crisis, debt risks and lack of urgency on reforms - Naidu
item.description: Fiji is facing critical water and sewage infrastructure gaps, rising debt vulnerabilities and slow progress on key economic reforms by the government, according to Fiscal Review Committee Chair Richard Naidu. Speaking during the State of the Fijian Economy Dialogue 2026, Naidu says there is a clear lack of urgency in addressing Fiji’s major economic challenges despite the availability of policy ad
item.content:encoded:
Fiji is facing critical water and sewage infrastructure gaps, rising debt vulnerabilities and slow progress on key economic reforms by the government, according to Fiscal Review Committee Chair Richard Naidu.

Speaking during the State of the Fijian Economy Dialogue 2026, Naidu says there is a clear lack of urgency in addressing Fiji’s major economic challenges despite the availability of policy advice, technical support and reform options.

He says while multiple solutions exist, what is missing is decisive action at the political level to address long-standing structural issues in the economy.

Naidu says water and sewage infrastructure remain one of Fiji’s most urgent national priorities and continue to be critically underfunded and underdeveloped.

He says enough has not been done to replicate the transformation of the former Fiji Electricity Authority into Energy Fiji Limited, which he says has become financially sustainable, operates with greater autonomy and has the capacity to invest for future expansion.

In contrast, Naidu says the Water Authority of Fiji remains heavily constrained, lacking financial autonomy and the ability to invest at the scale required to meet national needs.

He highlighted the low cost of water services, saying a person can pour about 1,400 bottles of water down a sink and pay only 15 cents, adding that the current pricing structure is not sufficient to sustain infrastructure upgrades.

Naidu says Fiji is still far from achieving a sustainable funding model for water services, warning that the sector remains one of the most neglected areas of infrastructure planning.

Beyond water, he warned that Fiji faces a broader triple threat of high debt levels, limited fiscal space to respond to shocks, and significant infrastructure demands that require billions in investment.

He says Fiji’s debt-to-GDP ratio limits the country’s ability to borrow or respond effectively to major crises such as cyclones, earthquakes or future pandemics.

Naidu says Fiji has been fortunate in recent years to avoid major natural disasters, but stressed that the country remains highly exposed and financially unprepared for a major shock.

He also pointed to global comparisons, noting that while Fiji has avoided severe events recently, neighbouring Pacific countries have experienced multiple Category 5 cyclones and earthquakes in the same period.

Naidu says this highlights the importance of building stronger fiscal buffers and ensuring preparedness for future emergencies.

He questioned whether Fiji’s current economic growth path is sufficient, saying growth below 3 percent is not adequate for long-term development needs and income growth.

Naidu says Fiji must avoid complacency in an increasingly competitive global economy, where stronger-performing countries can affect Fiji’s exchange rate, imports and overall cost of living.

He also raised concerns about the country’s tax base, saying Fiji has limited options for raising revenue and relies heavily on indirect taxation measures such as VAT.

Naidu says this makes fiscal decisions more constrained and reinforces the importance of economic growth as the main driver of government revenue.

On infrastructure, he says Fiji continues to lag in critical areas such as water, sewage, electricity, roads and bridges, many of which require urgent and large-scale investment.

Naidu also called for faster and more effective immigration reforms to address critical skills shortages that are slowing down major investment projects.

He says the country cannot move its growing investment pipeline forward without the necessary technical expertise.

He says Fiji needs engineers, builders, architects, accountants and other technical professionals to ensure projects are implemented efficiently and on time.

Naidu says reforming immigration settings to allow skilled workers into the country more quickly would help reduce bottlenecks and support private sector growth.

He says that addressing labour and skills constraints is a key part of creating an environment where businesses can invest, expand and create jobs.

item.title: No pay cut for civil servants in the budget - PM
item.description: Prime Minister, Sitiveni Rabuka stresses that there will be no pay cut for civil servants in the 2026/2027 National Budget.Rabuka

item.title: Taveuni villagers step in to help students after repeated yaqona thefts
item.description: Farmers in Taveuni continue to battle yaqona and dalo theft, as villagers say years of hard work can be wiped out overnight, affec
